We also saw strong fuel performance on both the margin and volume front. This reinforces the core advantage of Calumet's integrated model. .

With respect to Performance Brands, the company continues to see growth year over year despite selling a small industrial portion for $100 million earlier in the year. TRUFUEL now benefits from selling space of over 4000 Walmart stores.

The balance of the specialty and tradition refining is summarized in the next slide.

On the left of the slide, the company shows sales volume for both. The gray line is traditional refining. Notice the continued strength above 40,000 barrels per day. The blue line is specialty with a notable slight uptrend. The need for U.S. refining products remains strong, and with continued California and other refinery shutdowns. The spreads likely continue elevated. Over the past several years, significant capital was spent on improving operations plus operating practices. The results show a year-over-year savings at over $70 million. Some of it is in transportation. The margins for specialty remain seasonably strong even with business being mid-cycle.

MRL, the business arm for renewables, continues to experience short-term weakness, particularly with renewable diesel margins. The previous administration left RVO, production targets, low, while continuing to invite foreign dumping. Also, the 3rd quarter experienced "feedstock physical basis widened out, which means feedstocks were about $0.20 a gallon more expensive than the traditional CBOT marker would suggest across the industry." Since the September report, CBOT margins returned to more normal levels. Margins had been crushed. Yet, this business continues as the U.S. and likely world flagship lowest cost producer going forward. Notice the crashing cost of manufacturing created over the past few years in the following slide.

Much of the lower cost came from better management of wastewater. A significant element with MRL is location of cost effective and a variety of feed materials. This coupled, with a two week or less feed chain allows for optimal production execution. This was not an accident; it was designed in from the beginning. The $0.52 breakeven for MRL is industry leading. Also, both the traditional and renewable businesses remained combined for financial reporting. We expect this to change rather quickly in the 1st half of 2026.

An interesting comment on renewables seems more than in order:

Like we mentioned last quarter, our offtake is shaping up to be a diversified slate of direct physical customers, airlines, FBOs and Scope 3 customers of varying sizes, some of which are large multinationals who you might routinely envision when you think of carbon reduction initiatives and some of which are more boutique customers. In many ways, the SAF business highly resembles our Specialty Products business, where the ability to be flexible on logistics, go-to-market in varying ways to suit a wide range of customer needs and sell in all types of sizes make us preferred and differentiated supplier.

Long-term this has significance and illustrates a massive advantage in marketing and other important resource skills.

With respect to the Montana traditional business, it continues with a level of strength. For September, management noted,

On the Montana asphalt side, the third quarter is typically a good one. This quarter, in particular, we saw one of the strongest quarters in recent memory and a $14 million year-over-year gain. Our polymer modified asphalt business continues to be an advantage as well as the niche fuels distribution and with costs dramatically improved, we are pleased to see the impact on the bottom line this quarter.

Notice the asphalt positioning includes a meaningful lean toward specialty.

Depressed Renewable Market

Continuing, during the last conference, management noted the biomass-based diesel production "remains cut back at roughly 60% utilization." Additional color includes:

  • 2025 production seems now stable at 350 million gallons per month.
    • 4.5 billion gallons on a yearly basis.
  • One billion short of the 2025 targets.
  • D4 RIN purchases in the open market will eventually be required to cover this shortfall.
  • Shutdowns of high-cost producers continued.

2025 Update

On January 5th, management released a general business update in a press release. In bullet form, a summary follows:

  • EBITDA between $285 million - $305 million.
    • Past updates signaled close to or at the higher end of guidance, meaning $300 million plus is the likely result.
      • The EBITDA reported for the first three quarters equaled $223 million.
    • 4th Quarter EBITDA likely equals approximately $80 million, an approximate increase of $25 million year over year.
      • Also, 1st quarter last year equaled $55 or approximately the same as 4th quarter the prior year.

Shortly after the update, the company issued new bonds, extinguishing both the 2026 and 2027 issues. The issue placed $405 million at 9.75% due in 2031. The total 26s and 27s equaled $450 million. Management likely used $25 million of the $166 million cash on hand to finish the payoff. In all, interest expenses are lower by approximately $1 million per quarter.

Debt Structure

Of most concern for investors, it's the debt structure. Currently, the total debt equals $2.3 billion, of which $800 million is a DOE loan at 3-4% interest rates. The company has an approximately five-year period interest free. The latest capital structure is shown in the next slide.

The most recent capital changes minus the DOE loan is about $1.3 billion, net cash, or about $500 million above the company target of $800 million.

On the leverage front, a slide from the Goldman presentation reports a history.

Leverage is dropping but not fast enough for some investors.

We finished this; this is where the company stands today. Time to move forward with the future.

Come Together

The past is always important but often doesn't define the future. The company generated $300 million in EBITDA in 2025, a tall and important feat. Now it is time to discuss 2026 and beyond.

SAF margins and production rates define the major financial change. Management provides investors a five-year view.

We've discussed the chart on this slide before, which suggests that global supply and demand is largely balanced in 2025, and that turns to a supply deficit in 2026 as that gap grows each year as European mandates and other global mandates step up. Interestingly, early on, we received questions around this outlook, which really fit into 3 general categories. One, was will Europe really increase their volume mandate. Two, will voluntary demand grow; and 3, weren't we underestimating new supply.

The SAF slide eluted in the discussion follows.

Demand is expected to ramp slowly through 2029 with major demand increases in 2030. With this in mind, management noted European SAF prices have been on the rise, the region that continues mandatory usage, with prices up to $8 per gallon.

The Calumet approach sells via rail using the high-line to the West Coast, Midwest and Canada at an undisclosed premium to renewable diesel but is between $1 - $2 per gallon. For estimating purposes, we have used $1.5. Any of the listed markets could soak up the entire MRL production. Management plans an optimal approach, which now includes trucks into small regional airports.

In early April, a project at Great Falls comes online for an additional 100 million gallons. In the 2028 timeframe, a second SAF expansion startup is planned. Again, a slide shows the projected path.

The 300 million gallons project also increases the total output of MRL from the 12K barrels per day to 16K barrels per day.

So, what this mean? A table follows, illustrating the magnitude of the effect.

* $1.5 per gallon adder.

*** DGD indicators average 0.60 for all of 2025.

**** 12K barrels per day for 350 days.

@ We included yearly run rates in the table. Markets react to future possibilities and will likely value the entity on the run rate, not the actual yearly results.

With respect to the older businesses, management continues to forecast low-hanging fruit ranging in the 20% or so yearly return. Also, last year's 4th quarter report showed significant EBITDA growth year over year, $25 million. With 1st quarter generally similar to the 4th, an expectation of a $25 - $30 increase in EBITDA could be real.

In summary for 2026, the $300 million EBITDA results for 2025 could grow to $525 million to $550 million, almost doubling from 2025. For 2028, EBITDA could reach $900 million. The company's outstanding stock is about 85 million shares. We left out low-hanging specialty fruit.

Management continues beating the drum about asset sales. Products not covered with vertical integration could be sold. We aren't exactly sure what this means or which business, but this fact suggests positive surprises should be expected.

Risk

In our view, RINS, the amounts still in existence, might cause future issues. Thus far, the EPA hasn't given Calumet a full small refinery exception. The most recent report still shows $150 million in possible RIN exposure. Although Specialty has never paid cash for RINS, it must be considered until the courts once again rule in Calumet's favor. We believe that they will, but until it does, a watchful eye is recommended. Another risk, the economy could falter from lowering, in particular, fuel demand and crack spreads. But a possible economic recession seems remote to us.

Summary

This article sets in place markers for future financial results, results with a level of credibility. An almost doubling of the EBITDA, year over year, seems likely for 2026. It's transformative. In two more years, a triple might be in order. With approximately 85 million shares outstanding, $6 - $11 per share earnings is our estimate ($550 million divided by 85 million and $900 divided by 85 million). We rate Calumet a buy going forward with prices under $20. It's cheap. This year seems to be the transition year.
